# getHRF function using the registry (Minimal Version) -----

#' Get HRF by Name
#'
#' Retrieves an HRF by name from the registry and optionally applies decorators.
#' This provides a unified interface for creating both pre-defined HRF objects
#' and custom basis sets with specified parameters.
#'
#' @param name Character string specifying the HRF type. Options include:
#'   \itemize{
#'     \item \code{"spmg1"}, \code{"spmg2"}, \code{"spmg3"} - SPM canonical HRFs
#'     \item \code{"gamma"}, \code{"gaussian"} - Simple parametric HRFs
#'     \item \code{"fir"} - Finite Impulse Response basis
#'     \item \code{"bspline"} or \code{"bs"} - B-spline basis
#'     \item \code{"fourier"} - Fourier basis
#'     \item \code{"daguerre"} - Daguerre spherical basis
#'     \item \code{"tent"} - Tent (linear spline) basis
#'   }
#' @param nbasis Number of basis functions (for basis set types)
#' @param span Temporal window in seconds (default: 24)
#' @param lag Time lag in seconds to apply (default: 0)
#' @param width Block width for block designs (default: 0)
#' @param summate Whether to sum responses in block designs (default: TRUE)
#' @param normalize Whether to normalize the HRF (default: FALSE)
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to generator functions (e.g., \code{scale} for daguerre)
#' @return An HRF object
#' @details
#' For single HRF types (spmg1, gamma, gaussian), the function returns
#' pre-defined objects. For basis set types (fir, bspline, fourier, daguerre),
#' it calls the appropriate generator function with the specified parameters.
#' @examples
#' # Get pre-defined canonical HRF
#' canonical <- getHRF("spmg1")
#' 
#' # Create custom FIR basis with 20 bins
#' fir20 <- getHRF("fir", nbasis = 20, span = 30)
#' 
#' # Create B-spline basis with lag
#' bs_lag <- getHRF("bspline", nbasis = 8, lag = 2)
#' 
#' # Create blocked Gaussian HRF
#' block_gauss <- getHRF("gaussian", width = 5)
#' @export
getHRF <- function(name = "spmg1", # Default to spmg1
                   nbasis=5, span=24,
                   lag=0, width=0,
                   summate=TRUE, normalize=FALSE, ...) {

  key   <- match.arg(tolower(name), names(HRF_REGISTRY))
  entry <- HRF_REGISTRY[[key]]

  base <- if (inherits(entry, "HRF")) {
            entry # Use pre-defined object
      } else {
            # Call generator, passing nbasis, span, and any relevant ... args
            gen_args <- c(list(nbasis=as.integer(nbasis), span=span), list(...))
            # Only pass args the generator actually accepts
            valid_args <- gen_args[names(gen_args) %in% names(formals(entry))]
            do.call(entry, valid_args)
          }

  # Apply decorators
  if (width != 0) {
      stopifnot(width > 0)
      base <- block_hrf(base, width = width, summate = summate)
  }
  if (lag != 0) {
      base <- lag_hrf(base, lag = lag)
  }
  if (normalize) {
      base <- normalise_hrf(base)
  }

  attr(base, "name") <- key # Set name attribute to the matched registry key
  base
}

#' Evaluate an HRF Object
#'
#' This function evaluates a hemodynamic response function (HRF) object for a given set of time points (grid) and other parameters.
#' It handles both point evaluation (duration=0) and block evaluation (duration > 0).
#'
#' @param x The HRF object (inherits from `HRF` and `function`).
#' @param grid A numeric vector of time points at which to evaluate the HRF.
#' @param amplitude The scaling value for the event (default: 1).
#' @param duration The duration of the event (seconds). If > 0, the HRF is evaluated over this duration (default: 0).
#' @param precision The temporal resolution for evaluating responses when duration > 0 (default: 0.2).
#' @param summate Logical; whether the HRF response should accumulate over the duration (default: TRUE). If FALSE, the maximum response within the duration window is taken (currently only supported for single-basis HRFs).
#' @param normalize Logical; scale output so that the peak absolute value is 1 (default: FALSE). Applied *after* amplitude scaling and duration processing.
#' @param ... Additional arguments (unused).
#' @return A numeric vector or matrix of HRF values at the specified time points.
#' @examples
#' # Evaluate canonical HRF at specific times
#' times <- seq(0, 20, by = 0.5)
#' response <- evaluate(HRF_SPMG1, times)
#' 
#' # Evaluate with amplitude scaling
#' response_scaled <- evaluate(HRF_SPMG1, times, amplitude = 2)
#' 
#' # Evaluate with duration (block design)
#' response_block <- evaluate(HRF_SPMG1, times, duration = 5, summate = TRUE)
#' 
#' # Multi-basis HRF evaluation
#' response_multi <- evaluate(HRF_SPMG3, times)  # Returns 3-column matrix
#' @export
evaluate.HRF <- function(x, grid, amplitude = 1, duration = 0,
                         precision = .2, summate = TRUE, normalize = FALSE, ...) {

  # Validate inputs
  if (!is.numeric(grid) || length(grid) == 0 || anyNA(grid)) {
    stop("`grid` must be a non-empty numeric vector with no NA values.",
         call. = FALSE)
  }
  if (!is.numeric(precision) || length(precision) != 1 || is.na(precision) ||
      precision <= 0) {
    stop("`precision` must be a positive numeric value.", call. = FALSE)
  }

  # Base function incorporating amplitude
  base <- function(g) amplitude * x(g)

  # Evaluate based on duration
  out <- if (duration < precision) {
      # Point evaluation
    base(grid)
  } else {
      # Block evaluation
    offs <- seq(0, duration, by = precision)
      # Evaluate HRF at shifted time points for each offset
      # Use lapply to handle potential matrix output from multi-basis HRFs
      hlist <- lapply(offs, function(o) base(grid - o))
      
      # Check if the result for the first offset is a matrix (multi-basis)
      is_multi_basis <- is.matrix(hlist[[1]])
      
      if (is_multi_basis) {
          # Combine matrices (summation is standard for multi-basis)
          if (summate) {
             Reduce("+", hlist)
    } else {
             # Taking max per-basis-column across offsets is non-standard and complex.
             # Sticking to summation for multi-basis block designs.
             warning("summate=FALSE is not typically used with multi-basis HRFs during block evaluation. Using summation.", call. = FALSE)
             Reduce("+", hlist)
          }
      } else {
          # Single basis HRF: hlist contains vectors, bind them into a matrix
        hmat <- do.call(cbind, hlist)
          if (summate) {
              rowSums(hmat)
          } else {
              # For single basis, take the max across the duration window at each grid point
              apply(hmat, 1, max, na.rm = TRUE) 
              # Alternative: find which offset gives max? apply(hmat, 1, function(vals) vals[which.max(vals)])
          }
      }
  }

  # Apply normalization if requested, handling matrix/vector case
  if (normalize) {
      if (is.matrix(out)) {
          peaks <- apply(out, 2, function(col) max(abs(col), na.rm = TRUE))
          peaks[peaks == 0 | is.na(peaks)] <- 1
          out <- sweep(out, 2, peaks, "/")
      } else {
          peak_val <- max(abs(out), na.rm = TRUE)
          if (!is.na(peak_val) && peak_val != 0) out / peak_val else out
      }
  } else {
    out
  }
}

#' Plot an HRF Object
#'
#' @param x An HRF object
#' @param ... Additional arguments passed to plotting functions
#' @method plot HRF
#' @export
plot.HRF <- function(x, ...) {
  t <- seq(0, attr(x, "span"), by = 0.1)
  y <- evaluate(x, t)
  
  if (is.matrix(y)) {
    graphics::matplot(t, y, type = "l", xlab = "Time (s)", ylab = "Response", 
                      main = attr(x, "name"), ...)
    # Simple legend
    graphics::legend("topright", paste("Basis", 1:ncol(y)), lty = 1)
  } else {
    graphics::plot(t, y, type = "l", xlab = "Time (s)", ylab = "Response", 
                   main = attr(x, "name"), ...)
    peak_idx <- which.max(y)
    graphics::points(t[peak_idx], y[peak_idx], pch = 19, col = "red")
    graphics::text(t[peak_idx], y[peak_idx], sprintf("Peak: %.1fs", t[peak_idx]), 
                   pos = 3, offset = 0.5)
  }
}

    <Python><![CDATA[
      # Python implementation following semantic specification
      
      import numpy as np
      
      class HRF:
          """Simple HRF class for evaluation testing."""
          def __init__(self, func, name="hrf", nbasis=1, span=24):
              self.func = func
              self.name = name
              self.nbasis = nbasis
              self.span = span
          
          def __call__(self, t):
              return self.func(t)
      
      def evaluate_hrf(hrf, grid, amplitude=1, duration=0, precision=0.2, 
                       summate=True, normalize=False):
          """
          Evaluate HRF with duration-based convolution mechanics.
          
          Args:
              hrf: HRF object or function
              grid: Time points for evaluation
              amplitude: Scaling factor
              duration: Block duration (0 for point evaluation)
              precision: Temporal resolution for block evaluation
              summate: If True, sum over duration; if False, take max
              normalize: If True, normalize to peak absolute value of 1
              
          Returns:
              Array or matrix of HRF values
          """
          grid = np.asarray(grid, dtype=float)
          
          # Base function with amplitude scaling
          def base_func(g):
              return amplitude * hrf(g)
          
          # Evaluate based on duration
          if duration < precision:
              # Point evaluation
              out = base_func(grid)
          else:
              # Block evaluation
              offsets = np.arange(0, duration + precision/2, precision)
              
              # Evaluate HRF at shifted time points for each offset
              hrf_values = []
              for offset in offsets:
                  values = base_func(grid - offset)
                  hrf_values.append(values)
              
              # Check if multi-basis (matrix output)
              is_multi_basis = len(hrf_values[0].shape) > 1 and hrf_values[0].shape[1] > 1
              
              if is_multi_basis:
                  # Multi-basis: always use summation
                  if not summate:
                      import warnings
                      warnings.warn("summate=False is not typically used with multi-basis HRFs. Using summation.")
                  out = np.sum(hrf_values, axis=0)
              else:
                  # Single basis: stack into matrix and apply combination rule
                  hrf_matrix = np.column_stack(hrf_values)
                  if summate:
                      out = np.sum(hrf_matrix, axis=1)
                  else:
                      out = np.max(hrf_matrix, axis=1)
          
          # Apply normalization if requested
          if normalize:
              if len(out.shape) > 1:  # Matrix case
                  peaks = np.max(np.abs(out), axis=0)
                  peaks[peaks == 0] = 1
                  out = out / peaks
              else:  # Vector case
                  peak_val = np.max(np.abs(out))
                  if peak_val != 0:
                      out = out / peak_val
          
          return out
    ]]></Python>
    
    <Rust><![CDATA[
      // Rust implementation following semantic specification
      
      use ndarray::{Array1, Array2, Axis};
      
      pub struct HRF {
          pub func: fn(&[f64]) -> Array1<f64>,
          pub name: String,
          pub nbasis: usize,
          pub span: f64,
      }
      
      impl HRF {
          pub fn evaluate(&self, t: &[f64]) -> Array1<f64> {
              (self.func)(t)
          }
      }
      
      pub fn evaluate_hrf_duration(
          hrf: &HRF,
          grid: &[f64],
          amplitude: f64,
          duration: f64,
          precision: f64,
          summate: bool,
          normalize: bool,
      ) -> Array1<f64> {
          let grid_array = Array1::from_vec(grid.to_vec());
          
          let out = if duration < precision {
              // Point evaluation
              amplitude * hrf.evaluate(grid)
          } else {
              // Block evaluation
              let mut current_offset = 0.0;
              let mut hrf_values = Vec::new();
              
              while current_offset <= duration + precision / 2.0 {
                  let shifted_grid: Vec<f64> = grid.iter().map(|&t| t - current_offset).collect();
                  let values = amplitude * hrf.evaluate(&shifted_grid);
                  hrf_values.push(values);
                  current_offset += precision;
              }
              
              // Combine based on summate parameter
              let n_time = grid.len();
              let mut result = Array1::zeros(n_time);
              
              if summate {
                  // Sum across all offsets
                  for hrf_val in hrf_values {
                      result = result + hrf_val;
                  }
              } else {
                  // Take maximum across offsets
                  for i in 0..n_time {
                      let mut max_val = f64::NEG_INFINITY;
                      for hrf_val in &hrf_values {
                          max_val = max_val.max(hrf_val[i]);
                      }
                      result[i] = max_val;
                  }
              }
              
              result
          };
          
          // Apply normalization if requested
          if normalize {
              let peak_val = out.iter().map(|&x| x.abs()).fold(0.0, f64::max);
              if peak_val != 0.0 {
                  out / peak_val
              } else {
                  out
              }
          } else {
              out
          }
      }
      
      // Default parameters wrapper
      pub fn evaluate_hrf_default(hrf: &HRF, grid: &[f64]) -> Array1<f64> {
          evaluate_hrf_duration(hrf, grid, 1.0, 0.0, 0.2, true, false)
      }
    ]]></Rust>

      # Python implementation following semantic specification
      
      import numpy as np
      
      class HRF:
          """Simple HRF class for evaluation testing."""
          def __init__(self, func, name="hrf", nbasis=1, span=24):
              self.func = func
              self.name = name
              self.nbasis = nbasis
              self.span = span
          
          def __call__(self, t):
              return self.func(t)
      
      def evaluate_hrf(hrf, grid, amplitude=1, duration=0, precision=0.2, 
                       summate=True, normalize=False):
          """
          Evaluate HRF with duration-based convolution mechanics.
          
          Args:
              hrf: HRF object or function
              grid: Time points for evaluation
              amplitude: Scaling factor
              duration: Block duration (0 for point evaluation)
              precision: Temporal resolution for block evaluation
              summate: If True, sum over duration; if False, take max
              normalize: If True, normalize to peak absolute value of 1
              
          Returns:
              Array or matrix of HRF values
          """
          grid = np.asarray(grid, dtype=float)
          
          # Base function with amplitude scaling
          def base_func(g):
              return amplitude * hrf(g)
          
          # Evaluate based on duration
          if duration < precision:
              # Point evaluation
              out = base_func(grid)
          else:
              # Block evaluation
              offsets = np.arange(0, duration + precision/2, precision)
              
              # Evaluate HRF at shifted time points for each offset
              hrf_values = []
              for offset in offsets:
                  values = base_func(grid - offset)
                  hrf_values.append(values)
              
              # Check if multi-basis (matrix output)
              is_multi_basis = len(hrf_values[0].shape) > 1 and hrf_values[0].shape[1] > 1
              
              if is_multi_basis:
                  # Multi-basis: always use summation
                  if not summate:
                      import warnings
                      warnings.warn("summate=False is not typically used with multi-basis HRFs. Using summation.")
                  out = np.sum(hrf_values, axis=0)
              else:
                  # Single basis: stack into matrix and apply combination rule
                  hrf_matrix = np.column_stack(hrf_values)
                  if summate:
                      out = np.sum(hrf_matrix, axis=1)
                  else:
                      out = np.max(hrf_matrix, axis=1)
          
          # Apply normalization if requested
          if normalize:
              if len(out.shape) > 1:  # Matrix case
                  peaks = np.max(np.abs(out), axis=0)
                  peaks[peaks == 0] = 1
                  out = out / peaks
              else:  # Vector case
                  peak_val = np.max(np.abs(out))
                  if peak_val != 0:
                      out = out / peak_val
          
          return out
    ]]></Python>

      // Rust implementation following semantic specification
      
      use ndarray::{Array1, Array2, Axis};
      
      pub struct HRF {
          pub func: fn(&[f64]) -> Array1<f64>,
          pub name: String,
          pub nbasis: usize,
          pub span: f64,
      }
      
      impl HRF {
          pub fn evaluate(&self, t: &[f64]) -> Array1<f64> {
              (self.func)(t)
          }
      }
      
      pub fn evaluate_hrf_duration(
          hrf: &HRF,
          grid: &[f64],
          amplitude: f64,
          duration: f64,
          precision: f64,
          summate: bool,
          normalize: bool,
      ) -> Array1<f64> {
          let grid_array = Array1::from_vec(grid.to_vec());
          
          let out = if duration < precision {
              // Point evaluation
              amplitude * hrf.evaluate(grid)
          } else {
              // Block evaluation
              let mut current_offset = 0.0;
              let mut hrf_values = Vec::new();
              
              while current_offset <= duration + precision / 2.0 {
                  let shifted_grid: Vec<f64> = grid.iter().map(|&t| t - current_offset).collect();
                  let values = amplitude * hrf.evaluate(&shifted_grid);
                  hrf_values.push(values);
                  current_offset += precision;
              }
              
              // Combine based on summate parameter
              let n_time = grid.len();
              let mut result = Array1::zeros(n_time);
              
              if summate {
                  // Sum across all offsets
                  for hrf_val in hrf_values {
                      result = result + hrf_val;
                  }
              } else {
                  // Take maximum across offsets
                  for i in 0..n_time {
                      let mut max_val = f64::NEG_INFINITY;
                      for hrf_val in &hrf_values {
                          max_val = max_val.max(hrf_val[i]);
                      }
                      result[i] = max_val;
                  }
              }
              
              result
          };
          
          // Apply normalization if requested
          if normalize {
              let peak_val = out.iter().map(|&x| x.abs()).fold(0.0, f64::max);
              if peak_val != 0.0 {
                  out / peak_val
              } else {
                  out
              }
          } else {
              out
          }
      }
      
      // Default parameters wrapper
      pub fn evaluate_hrf_default(hrf: &HRF, grid: &[f64]) -> Array1<f64> {
          evaluate_hrf_duration(hrf, grid, 1.0, 0.0, 0.2, true, false)
      }

From what I saw, the empirical_hrf function uses stats::approxfun with specific parameters:
```r
empirical_hrf <- function(t, y, name = "empirical_hrf") {
  as_hrf(stats::approxfun(t, y, yright = 0, yleft = 0),
         name = name, nbasis = 1L, span = max(t, na.rm = TRUE))
}
```

This creates an interpolating function that:
- Returns 0 for t < min(t) (yleft = 0)
- Returns 0 for t > max(t) (yright = 0) 
- Interpolates linearly between provided data points
- Uses max(t) as the span

      # Python implementation following semantic specification
      
      import numpy as np
      from scipy.interpolate import interp1d
      
      def empirical_hrf(t, y, name="empirical_hrf"):
          """
          Create empirical HRF from data points using linear interpolation.
          
          Args:
              t: Time points (array-like)
              y: Response values (array-like)
              name: Name for the HRF (default: "empirical_hrf")
              
          Returns:
              HRF object with interpolation function
          """
          t = np.asarray(t, dtype=float)
          y = np.asarray(y, dtype=float)
          
          if len(t) != len(y):
              raise ValueError("t and y must have the same length")
          
          if len(t) == 0:
              raise ValueError("t and y cannot be empty")
          
          # Calculate span
          span = np.max(t)
          
          # Create interpolation function with boundary conditions
          if len(t) == 1:
              # Single point case
              def interp_func(eval_t):
                  eval_t = np.asarray(eval_t, dtype=float)
                  result = np.zeros_like(eval_t)
                  mask = eval_t == t[0]
                  result[mask] = y[0]
                  return result
          else:
              # Multiple points case
              # Use linear interpolation with bounds_error=False and fill_value=0
              interp = interp1d(t, y, kind='linear', bounds_error=False, 
                               fill_value=0.0, assume_sorted=False)
              
              def interp_func(eval_t):
                  eval_t = np.asarray(eval_t, dtype=float)
                  return interp(eval_t)
          
          # Create HRF-like object
          class EmpiricalHRF:
              def __init__(self, func, name, span):
                  self.func = func
                  self.name = name
                  self.span = span
                  self.nbasis = 1
              
              def __call__(self, t):
                  return self.func(t)
              
              def evaluate(self, t):
                  return self.func(t)
          
          return EmpiricalHRF(interp_func, name, span)
    ]]></Python>
    
    <Rust><![CDATA[
      // Rust implementation following semantic specification
      
      use std::collections::HashMap;
      
      #[derive(Clone)]
      pub struct EmpiricalHRF {
          pub time_points: Vec<f64>,
          pub response_values: Vec<f64>,
          pub name: String,
          pub span: f64,
          pub nbasis: usize,
      }
      
      impl EmpiricalHRF {
          pub fn new(t: Vec<f64>, y: Vec<f64>, name: Option<String>) -> Result<Self, String> {
              if t.len() != y.len() {
                  return Err("t and y must have the same length".to_string());
              }
              
              if t.is_empty() {
                  return Err("t and y cannot be empty".to_string());
              }
              
              let span = t.iter().fold(f64::NEG_INFINITY, |acc, &x| acc.max(x));
              
              Ok(EmpiricalHRF {
                  time_points: t,
                  response_values: y,
                  name: name.unwrap_or_else(|| "empirical_hrf".to_string()),
                  span,
                  nbasis: 1,
              })
          }
          
          pub fn evaluate(&self, eval_t: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
              eval_t.iter().map(|&t| self.interpolate_at(t)).collect()
          }
          
          fn interpolate_at(&self, t: f64) -> f64 {
              // Handle boundary conditions first
              if t < self.time_points[0] || t > self.time_points[self.time_points.len() - 1] {
                  return 0.0;
              }
              
              // Single point case
              if self.time_points.len() == 1 {
                  return if t == self.time_points[0] { self.response_values[0] } else { 0.0 };
              }
              
              // Find interpolation interval
              for i in 0..self.time_points.len() - 1 {
                  if t >= self.time_points[i] && t <= self.time_points[i + 1] {
                      // Linear interpolation
                      let t1 = self.time_points[i];
                      let t2 = self.time_points[i + 1];
                      let y1 = self.response_values[i];
                      let y2 = self.response_values[i + 1];
                      
                      if t1 == t2 {
                          return y1; // Avoid division by zero
                      }
                      
                      return y1 + (t - t1) * (y2 - y1) / (t2 - t1);
                  }
              }
              
              // Exact match with last point
              if t == self.time_points[self.time_points.len() - 1] {
                  return self.response_values[self.response_values.len() - 1];
              }
              
              0.0 // Should not reach here, but return 0 as fallback
          }
      }
      
      // Constructor function
      pub fn empirical_hrf(t: Vec<f64>, y: Vec<f64>, name: Option<String>) -> Result<EmpiricalHRF, String> {
          EmpiricalHRF::new(t, y, name)
      }
    ]]></Rust>

    <purpose>Validate empirical HRF implementation with data-driven interpolation, boundary conditions, and cross-language interpolation consistency</purpose>
    <algorithm>
      The empirical_hrf function creates HRF objects from user-provided data points:
      
      **Core Implementation:**
      empirical_hrf(t, y, name) = as_hrf(stats::approxfun(t, y, yright=0, yleft=0), ...)
      
      **Interpolation Method:**
      - Uses linear interpolation between provided (t, y) data points
      - Built on stats::approxfun with specific boundary conditions
      - Ensures continuous but not necessarily smooth HRF
      
      **Boundary Conditions:**
      - yleft = 0: Returns 0 for time points t < min(data_t)
      - yright = 0: Returns 0 for time points t > max(data_t)
      - Enforces causal HRF (zero outside data domain)
      
      **Span Calculation:**
      - span = max(t, na.rm = TRUE)
      - Automatically determined from data range
      - Used for HRF object metadata
      
      **Key Properties:**
      - Single basis function (nbasis = 1)
      - Linear interpolation preserves data points exactly
      - Zero-padding outside data domain
      - Handles arbitrary sampling patterns in input data
    </algorithm>
    <mathematical_properties>
      **Interpolation Properties:**
      - Piecewise linear function between data points
      - Continuous but not differentiable at data points
      - Exact preservation of input (t, y) pairs
      - Linear extrapolation within domain
      
      **Boundary Behavior:**
      - f(t) = 0 for t < min(data_t)
      - f(t) = 0 for t > max(data_t)
      - f(data_t[i]) = data_y[i] exactly
      - Linear interpolation: f(t) = y[i] + (t - t[i]) * (y[i+1] - y[i]) / (t[i+1] - t[i])
      
      **Cross-language Considerations:**
      - Linear interpolation algorithms may differ slightly
      - Boundary condition implementation must be consistent
      - Floating-point precision in interpolation
      - Extrapolation behavior at domain edges
      
      **Data Requirements:**
      - Requires at least 1 data point
      - t values should be sorted (though approxfun handles unsorted)
      - Handles duplicate t values by taking first y value
      - NaN/Inf handling depends on underlying interpolation
    </mathematical_properties>
    <edge_cases>
      - Single data point: Should create constant function within boundaries
      - Duplicate t values: Should use first y value or handle gracefully
      - Unsorted t values: Should work (approxfun sorts automatically)
      - t = 0 exactly: Should handle boundary correctly
      - Very sparse data: Should interpolate with large linear segments
      - Negative y values: Should preserve sign through interpolation
      - Large time spans: Should handle efficiently
    </edge_cases>

      # Python implementation following semantic specification
      
      import numpy as np
      from scipy.interpolate import interp1d
      
      def empirical_hrf(t, y, name="empirical_hrf"):
          """
          Create empirical HRF from data points using linear interpolation.
          
          Args:
              t: Time points (array-like)
              y: Response values (array-like)
              name: Name for the HRF (default: "empirical_hrf")
              
          Returns:
              HRF object with interpolation function
          """
          t = np.asarray(t, dtype=float)
          y = np.asarray(y, dtype=float)
          
          if len(t) != len(y):
              raise ValueError("t and y must have the same length")
          
          if len(t) == 0:
              raise ValueError("t and y cannot be empty")
          
          # Calculate span
          span = np.max(t)
          
          # Create interpolation function with boundary conditions
          if len(t) == 1:
              # Single point case
              def interp_func(eval_t):
                  eval_t = np.asarray(eval_t, dtype=float)
                  result = np.zeros_like(eval_t)
                  mask = eval_t == t[0]
                  result[mask] = y[0]
                  return result
          else:
              # Multiple points case
              # Use linear interpolation with bounds_error=False and fill_value=0
              interp = interp1d(t, y, kind='linear', bounds_error=False, 
                               fill_value=0.0, assume_sorted=False)
              
              def interp_func(eval_t):
                  eval_t = np.asarray(eval_t, dtype=float)
                  return interp(eval_t)
          
          # Create HRF-like object
          class EmpiricalHRF:
              def __init__(self, func, name, span):
                  self.func = func
                  self.name = name
                  self.span = span
                  self.nbasis = 1
              
              def __call__(self, t):
                  return self.func(t)
              
              def evaluate(self, t):
                  return self.func(t)
          
          return EmpiricalHRF(interp_func, name, span)
    ]]></Python>
    
    <Rust><![CDATA[
      // Rust implementation following semantic specification
      
      use std::collections::HashMap;
      
      #[derive(Clone)]
      pub struct EmpiricalHRF {
          pub time_points: Vec<f64>,
          pub response_values: Vec<f64>,
          pub name: String,
          pub span: f64,
          pub nbasis: usize,
      }
      
      impl EmpiricalHRF {
          pub fn new(t: Vec<f64>, y: Vec<f64>, name: Option<String>) -> Result<Self, String> {
              if t.len() != y.len() {
                  return Err("t and y must have the same length".to_string());
              }
              
              if t.is_empty() {
                  return Err("t and y cannot be empty".to_string());
              }
              
              let span = t.iter().fold(f64::NEG_INFINITY, |acc, &x| acc.max(x));
              
              Ok(EmpiricalHRF {
                  time_points: t,
                  response_values: y,
                  name: name.unwrap_or_else(|| "empirical_hrf".to_string()),
                  span,
                  nbasis: 1,
              })
          }
          
          pub fn evaluate(&self, eval_t: &[f64]) -> Vec<f64> {
              eval_t.iter().map(|&t| self.interpolate_at(t)).collect()
          }
          
          fn interpolate_at(&self, t: f64) -> f64 {
              // Handle boundary conditions first
              if t < self.time_points[0] || t > self.time_points[self.time_points.len() - 1] {
                  return 0.0;
              }
              
              // Single point case
              if self.time_points.len() == 1 {
                  return if t == self.time_points[0] { self.response_values[0] } else { 0.0 };
              }
              
              // Find interpolation interval
              for i in 0..self.time_points.len() - 1 {
                  if t >= self.time_points[i] && t <= self.time_points[i + 1] {
                      // Linear interpolation
                      let t1 = self.time_points[i];
                      let t2 = self.time_points[i + 1];
                      let y1 = self.response_values[i];
                      let y2 = self.response_values[i + 1];
                      
                      if t1 == t2 {
                          return y1; // Avoid division by zero
                      }
                      
                      return y1 + (t - t1) * (y2 - y1) / (t2 - t1);
                  }
              }
              
              // Exact match with last point
              if t == self.time_points[self.time_points.len() - 1] {
                  return self.response_values[self.response_values.len() - 1];
              }
              
              0.0 // Should not reach here, but return 0 as fallback
          }
      }
      
      // Constructor function
      pub fn empirical_hrf(t: Vec<f64>, y: Vec<f64>, name: Option<String>) -> Result<EmpiricalHRF, String> {
          EmpiricalHRF::new(t, y, name)
      }
